did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an improved gray wolf-based multiple-layer perceptron (IGWO-MLP) neural network for the automated detection and classification of skin diseases (melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, eczema, actinic keratoses, nevus, and lupus erythematosus). It employs feature extraction methods (NCLDM and ABCD-fv) and classification algorithms on six dermatological datasets (DermNet, Xiangya, Medicine Net, PH2, Kaggle, and HAM-10000).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work, including the proposal of a novel method (IGWO-MLP), feature extraction techniques (NCLDM and ABCD-fv), and performance evaluation on six specific datasets (DermNet, Xiangya, Medicine Net, PH2, Kaggle, and HAM-10000) with reported metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

title: Automated detection and classification of skin diseases using diverse features and improved gray wolf-based multiple-layer perceptron neural network
journal: International Journal of Imaging Systems and Technology
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated detection and classification system for six skin diseases: melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, eczema, actinic keratoses, nevus, and lupus erythematosus. The methodology employs preprocessing with gray world color constancy and morphological operations, followed by feature extraction using novel cumulative-based level difference mean (NCLDM) and improved ABCD feature vectors. Classification is performed using an improved gray wolf-based multiple-layer perceptron (IGWO-MLP) neural network. The model was evaluated on six datasets: DermNet, Xiangya, Medicine Net, PH2, Kaggle, and HAM-10000. The proposed method achieved 98% accuracy, 99% sensitivity, and 98% specificity, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in segmentation and classification tasks.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease class (melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, eczema, actinic keratoses, nevus, and lupus erythematosus)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes an improved gray wolf-based multiple-layer perceptron (IGWO-MLP) for the automated detection and classification of six specific skin diseases. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using feature extraction methods (NCLDM and ABCD-fv) on six datasets (DermNet, Xiangya, Medicine Net, PH2, Kaggle, and HAM-10000). Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics for class prediction.

The paper also performs skin lesion segmentation using morphological operations to segment the lesion region from the background. This is evaluated using Jaccard similarity index and Dice similarity index, with reported segmentation accuracy of 0.978.

datasets_used.name: DermNet
datasets_used.description: One of the largest online skin disease medical datasets. Contains 23,000 images of skin disease.

use.training: Used for training the IGWO-MLP model (85% of dataset, 5300 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the IGWO-MLP model (15% of dataset, 1300 images).

datasets_used.name: Xiangya
datasets_used.description: Dataset captured using digital camera, consisting of 150,250 skin images with 543 kinds of skin diseases.

use.training: Used for training the IGWO-MLP model (75% of dataset, 4600 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the IGWO-MLP model (25% of dataset, 1800 images).

datasets_used.name: Medicine Net
datasets_used.description: An online dataset with approximately 25,000 skin disease images.

use.training: Used for training the IGWO-MLP model (65% of dataset, 3300 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the IGWO-MLP model (35% of dataset, 2500 images).

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A benchmarking and research dataset for dermoscopic images, commonly used for segmentation and classification.

use.training: Used for training the IGWO-MLP model (90% of dataset, 7200 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the IGWO-MLP model (10% of dataset, 1010 images).

datasets_used.name: Kaggle
datasets_used.description: Dataset consisting of 11,020 skin disease images with multiple numbers of lesions.

use.training: Used for training the IGWO-MLP model (60% of dataset, 3900 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the IGWO-MLP model (40% of dataset, 2250 images).

datasets_used.name: HAM-10000
datasets_used.description: Dataset consisting of 10,000 images of 267 classes of skin diseases.

use.training: Used for training the IGWO-MLP model (55% of dataset, 2100 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the IGWO-MLP model (45% of dataset, 1250 images).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, Jaccard similarity index, and Dice similarity index, and compared qualitatively with state-of-the-art methods.

architecture_details.model_type: Multiple-Layer Perceptron (MLP) neural network optimized by Gray Wolf Optimization (GWO) with Levy flights
architecture_details.layers: Input layer, hidden layers (nonlinear activation function), output layer (linear function)
architecture_details.activation_functions: Nonlinear role for hidden layers; linear function for output layer
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Novel Cumulative-based Level Difference Mean (NCLDM) and improved Asymmetry, Border Irregularity, Color Variation and Diameter (ABCD) feature vector (ABCD-fv)
architecture_details.optimization_algorithm: Improved Gray Wolf Optimization (IGWO) used to set weights and biases

training_details.training_schedule: Training continued until maximum iteration and optimal disease classification was achieved
training_details.implementation_environment: MATLAB R2017b on Intel Core 3.91 GHz

classification_or_output_head.output: Skin disease class (Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Eczema, Actinic Keratoses, Nevus, Lupus Erythematosus)

preprocessing_techniques: Gray world color constancy (GWCC) algorithm for color standardization
Morphological operations (bottom-hat filtering) for removal of thick and thin hair artifacts
Structuring element (SE) with specific diameters (4-pixel for thin hair, 6-pixel or 8-pixel for thick hair)
Morphological closing operations with circular kernel (SE diameter 8-pixel) for segmentation
Binary mask application to remove dark corners and define lesion center
Border detection using circle diameter of two pixels with SE for eroding segmented image
Temporal image normalization to increase dynamic ranges
Resizing of input images to 525 x 525 pixels (from original 545 x 750)
conclusions: The proposed IGWO-MLP method achieves high performance in automated skin disease detection and classification, yielding 98% accuracy, 99% sensitivity, and 98% specificity across six datasets (DermNet, Xiangya, Medicine Net, PH2, Kaggle, and HAM-10000).

The combination of novel cumulative-based level difference mean (NCLDM) and improved ABCD feature vector (ABCD-fv) methods effectively extracts shape, texture, and color features for skin lesion classification.

The proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both segmentation and classification tasks, with segmentation metrics of 0.978 accuracy, 0.956 specificity, 0.967 sensitivity, 0.983 Jaccard similarity index, and 0.989 Dice similarity index.

In classification comparisons, the IGWO-MLP method (98% accuracy, 99% specificity, 98% sensitivity) surpasses baselines including DCNN (96% accuracy), CNN (89% accuracy), Naive Bayes (88% accuracy), and SVM (91% accuracy).

The use of improved gray wolf optimization (IGWO) with Levy flights enhances the convergence and performance of the multiple-layer perceptron (MLP) neural network compared to conventional GWO and other metaheuristic algorithms.
The study demonstrates that the proposed system can assist dermatologists in making their work more efficient and providing correct treatment for detected skin diseases.
important_remarks: The study evaluates six distinct skin diseases (melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, eczema, actinic keratoses, nevus, and lupus erythematosus) but assigns each disease to a separate dataset rather than using multi-class classification within a single dataset, which limits the assessment of inter-class discrimination capabilities.

The datasets used (DermNet, Xiangya, Medicine Net, PH2, Kaggle, HAM-10000) are heterogeneous, varying in acquisition devices, lighting conditions, and image resolutions, introducing significant domain bias and potential data leakage risks if preprocessing steps like color standardization inadvertently share statistical properties across splits.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance comparisons against state-of-the-art methods, relying solely on point estimates of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

The training and testing splits vary significantly across datasets (ranging from 85/15 to 55/45), making direct comparison of model robustness and generalization across different disease classes difficult.

The proposed method relies on hand-crafted feature extraction (NCLDM and ABCD-fv) combined with a shallow neural network (MLP), which may lack the representational power of deep learning models for complex, high-dimensional dermatological images, despite reported high accuracy.

The implementation is limited to MATLAB R2017b on a specific hardware configuration (Intel Core 3.91 GHz), and no code or pre-trained models are provided, hindering reproducibility and external validation.

The paper claims the proposed method outperforms deep learning baselines (DCNN, CNN) but does not provide detailed architectural specifications or hyperparameter tuning details for these baselines, raising concerns about the fairness of the comparison.
